title: Energy Transport in Randomly Coupled Quantum Systems: A Perturbative Approach
abstract: We study energy transport between two quantum systems coupled through a random interaction. The central feature of our approach is to model the coupling as a Gaussian random matrix, which enables a simple and systematic perturbative expansion. In the large-$N$ limit, we derive explicit expressions for the energy transfer rate and heat conductance up to second order in the coupling strength. Using spectral methods and diagrammatic expansions, we obtain the leading- and next-to-leading-order contributions to the energy transfer rate. We illustrate our results through explicit calculations for Gaussian, constant, semicircular, and Gamma densities of states.
summary: This paper develops a perturbative framework to calculate energy transport and heat conductance between two quantum systems coupled by random interactions. By expanding the energy current up to second order, the authors derive quantitative results for various spectral density models. The findings provide deep insights into how disorder affects thermalization rates in many-body quantum physics.
main_problem: The study focuses on quantifying energy transport, specifically heat conductance, between two quantum systems coupled by a random interaction.
main_result: Explicit expressions for the energy transfer rate and heat conductance are derived up to second order in the coupling strength for various spectral density models (Gaussian, constant, semicircular, Gamma).
method: The analysis employs perturbative expansions in the coupling strength, utilizing diagrammatic techniques and spectral methods within the large-$N$ limit.
model_or_system: The model consists of two quantum subsystems coupled by a random interaction matrix, analyzed using an initial thermal state assumption. The Hamiltonian structure allows for the decomposition of energy current into work and heat components.
key_observables: Energy current ($\dot{E}_1(t)$), Heat conductance ($\sigma$), Work current ($\dot{W}$), and Heat current ($\dot{Q}$).
important_parameters: Coupling strength ($J$), inverse temperatures ($ eta_1,  eta_2$), and the spectral density function $ho(\epsilon)$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on treating the coupling as a Gaussian random matrix ensemble and performing calculations in the thermodynamic limit ($N_1, N_2 	o \infty$).
figures_summary: Figures compare perturbative predictions (leading order + second-order correction) against numerical simulations for energy change rate ($\dot{E}_1$) and energy ($E_1$), demonstrating convergence behavior.
paper_structure: The paper systematically develops the calculation of the energy current using time evolution operators, derives general expressions up to second order in perturbation theory, and then applies these methods to calculate $\sigma$ for specific spectral distributions (Gaussian, Constant, Semicircle, Gamma).
why_interesting: This work provides rigorous theoretical tools to calculate non-equilibrium transport coefficients like heat conductance in complex quantum systems, which is highly relevant for understanding thermalization dynamics in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic of the model. The gray background represents the environment, which exchanges work currents ˙W1 and ˙W2 with the two subsystems. The quantity ˙Q denotes the heat current between the subsystems.

Figure 2: Schematic of the three pairing types. We use directed lines to represent matrix indices: an arrow pointing toward an operator corresponds to a row index, while an arrow pointing away from an operator corresponds to a column index. The translation of a diagram into a product of traces is straightforward. Begin at any operator and follow the direction of the arrows. Upon encountering a T, jump to the paired T along the dashed line, and then continue following the arrows until a closed loop is completed. This loop corresponds to a single trace, with the operators appearing in the order encountered along the path. Repeat this procedure until all operators in the diagram have been...
Figure 3: Energy current and integrated energy flux for the Gaussian model with J = 1 and E = 1. We set β2 = 1 in the left panel and β1 = 1 in the right panel.
Figure 4: Heat conductance for the Gaussian model and the constant distribution, with J = E = 1.

Figure 6: Comparison between perturbation theory and numerical simulation for the energy E1 and its time derivative ˙E1 of subsystem 1. Three coupling strengths are shown: J = 0.2, 0.5, and 0.8 (from top to bottom). The perturbation curves include the leading-order result and the result with second-order correction. Numerical data are averaged over 100 realizations of the random coupling. Model parameters: N1 = N2 = 10, β1 = 2, β2 = 1, and both subsystem spectra are drawn from a standard Gaussian distribution (E = 1).
